---
number-sections: false
---

# A Note on AI Assistance {.unnumbered}

Every chapter of this book was researched, written, and rewritten by me. I also used AI tools throughout. This note is required: MIT Press asks every author who uses AI to disclose its role. What follows is more than disclosure, though. It is also a statement of the standard I held the book to.

Every quantitative claim in these pages is traceable to source code that produces it. Every citation has been verified against its primary source. Every cross-reference resolves to a stable anchor. Every number in prose was computed, not typed. AI tools made parts of this rigor mechanically tractable. They did not certify any of it.

In practice, AI helped me brainstorm framings I then chose between, survey literature I then read in the original, draft passages I then rewrote substantially, and audit the manuscript for cross-reference and notation consistency. The pattern repeats: the tool proposes, the human ratifies. I read every source AI surfaced before keeping it, because hallucination is real and an unverified citation has no place in a textbook. The intellectual framework, the choice of examples, the chapter-to-chapter argument, and the decision of what to include and what to leave out are mine. The errors that remain are mine too.

This pattern is older than the present moment. In 1683 Joseph Moxon printed the first English-language manual on printing, *Mechanick Exercises on the Whole Art of Printing*, using the press it described. Three centuries later Donald Knuth wrote *The TeXbook* in TeX, the typesetting system he created so his own books could exist. The toolmaker documents the tool with the tool. To do otherwise here would be to teach about infrastructure I had chosen to avoid.

The verification gap I describe in chapter 1 of vol1 (that ML systems cannot be certified the way traditional logic can) applies to AI-assisted authoring with equal force. The standard I held this book to is the standard I want you to demand of every system you ship: the tool can propose, but only an engineer can ratify. MIT Press does not list AI tools as authors because they cannot assume ethical and legal responsibility for their work. The same is true of every engineered system. Authorship is the assumption of that responsibility, and it belongs to the human.
